## SECTION 1 — Global Pulse (Geopolitics & World Economy)
Geopolitical tensions in the Gulf region remain a focal point as the United States has renewed attacks on Iran, with explosions reported in southern regions including Sirik, Qeshm Island, Bandar Abbas, and Kharg Island. These strikes, reportedly in response to Iranian attacks on three commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz, have prompted Iran's military to pledge a "crushing response," leading to retaliatory drone and missile fire on U.S. military sites in Bahrain and Kuwait. UN Secretary-General Antonio Guterres has expressed alarm over these renewed military confrontations and called for de-escalation, urging all parties to exercise maximum restraint. Concurrently, global growth is projected to slow to 3.0 percent in 2026 before recovering to 3.4 percent in 2027, according to the International Monetary Fund. The Bank of the Lao PDR has implemented five priority measures to curb inflation and maintain an average inflation rate below 7 percent in 2026. India and Australia are expected to finalize a uranium export agreement during Prime Minister Narendra Modi's visit to Australia, marking a significant step in bilateral civil nuclear energy cooperation.

## SECTION 2 — Global News (Complete Continental Coverage)
### Americas
The United States has reportedly continued its strikes against Iran for a second straight day, aiming to degrade Iran's ability to threaten navigation in the Strait of Hormuz. Separately, Venezuela is grappling with the aftermath of recent earthquakes, with acting President Delcy Rodriguez expressing gratitude for humanitarian aid from 28 countries. Four people were killed in Mexico City during celebrations following Mexico's victory in the 2026 FIFA World Cup knockout round.

### Europe
Ongoing military confrontations in the Gulf region have led to increased calls for de-escalation by the UN Secretary-General. Wildfires are pushing Southern Europe into a fire emergency, exacerbating environmental concerns. An apartment block fire in Antwerp, Belgium, resulted in five fatalities and several injuries. The Esports World Cup has opened its third edition in Paris, France.

### Asia-Pacific
Typhoon Bavi is anticipated to skirt northern Taiwan before making landfall in China's eastern Fujian province on Saturday evening, July 11, potentially becoming the most destructive tropical storm in years for Taiwan. Japan Airlines and ANA have cancelled numerous domestic flights scheduled for Friday due to the typhoon. China is also still recovering from Typhoon Maysak, which caused floods killing 39 people and leaving nine missing in Guangxi. Chinese and Russian naval vessels have commenced live-fire maritime drills near Qingdao, China. China's recent submarine-launched ballistic missile test in the South Pacific has raised concerns among leaders in the Commonwealth of Northern Mariana Islands, underscoring the importance of regional peace and stability.

### Africa
The Democratic Republic of Congo has begun treatment trials for Ebola as part of efforts to combat the ongoing epidemic. In Sudan, human rights groups report that 15 people have been killed in drone strikes in North Kordofan state, and 10 people died in a drone strike near Omdurman while traveling to a wedding. The Sudanese Army has recaptured the Blue Nile town of Kurmuk. Uganda has confirmed a Marburg virus case in a toddler in the Kyegegwa District during Ebola surveillance.

## SECTION 3 — Gulf & MENA Region
The Middle East remains highly volatile, with the United States conducting a third straight day of strikes in Iran, targeting locations such as Bushehr and Konarak. This follows earlier U.S. strikes on over 80 sites in Iran and the revocation of a sanctions waiver allowing Iranian oil sales after July 7. Iran's missile attacks have renewed risks in the Strait of Hormuz. Air raid sirens were activated in Bahrain and Kuwait as U.S. military sites reportedly came under Iranian drone and missile fire in retaliation. Israel's Defence Minister, Israel Katz, has stated that Israel is prepared to launch further strikes against Iran if deemed necessary. UN Secretary-General Antonio Guterres has expressed serious concerns over the renewed military confrontations in the Gulf and urged de-escalation. The UAE has welcomed the U.S. President's announcement to initiate the removal of Syria from the State Sponsors of Terrorism List, viewing it as a positive step for Syria's recovery and integration into the global economy.

## SECTION 13 — India National (Politics & Governance)
India and Australia are expected to finalize a uranium export agreement during Prime Minister Narendra Modi's visit, marking a significant step in bilateral cooperation on civil nuclear energy. Union Minister Nitin Gadkari has stated that using E20 petrol may reduce vehicle mileage but will not damage engines. The Government of Jammu and Kashmir has directed schools and coaching centers to identify and remove books containing objectionable content as part of a review of educational material. India has slipped one place to 125th in the Global Passport Index 2026, despite recording its highest overall passport score in five years. Six people, including five children, died after a dilapidated residential building collapsed in Mumbai amidst torrential monsoon rain, leading to a red weather alert. Two Assam Rifles personnel were killed on July 7 in a militant ambush in Manipur's Ukhrul district. Sourabh Chandrakar, founder of the Mahadev betting app syndicate, has been detained in Oman based on an Interpol Red Notice; Indian authorities are seeking his extradition or deportation in connection with alleged proceeds of crime worth ₹6,000 crore. The Delhi High Court directed the Central government to restore the X account of the youth-led Cockroach Janta Party, which had been blocked due to posts related to a leaked medical entrance examination. The Natural History Museum at Mizoram University in Aizawl has been designated India's 21st official biodiversity repository under the Biological Diversity Act. The Congress party has called for the resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan and the disbandment of the National Testing Agency (NTA) following alleged irregularities in the UGC-NET exam.

---

## SECTION 14 — Telangana State Updates
Telangana Chief Minister A. Revanth Reddy inaugurated Premier Energies' Next-Gen Solar Module Manufacturing Facility in Seetharampur, Rangareddy district on Thursday, July 9, outlining the "Telangana Rising 2047" vision to contribute 10% to India's GDP by 2047. Left-affiliated student organizations have called for a statewide bandh (strike) of all educational institutions on Friday, July 10, to demand solutions for various issues in the education sector. Many private schools and colleges in Telangana have declared a holiday in anticipation of the bandh, potentially leading to a three-day closure for some institutions when combined with the weekend. Student organizations allege that the state government plans to reduce the number of government schools from approximately 27,000 to 4,000 and demand this proposal be dropped. Key demands by the student unions include the immediate release of an estimated ₹10,000 crore in pending scholarships and fee-reimbursement dues to colleges across Telangana. The organizations also seek the appointment of a dedicated Education Minister, the immediate filling of vacant teacher posts, and the construction of permanent buildings for government schools, hostels, and Gurukul institutions. "Chalo Collectorate" protests were organized by the Students Federation of India (SFI) Telangana and allied student organizations earlier this week in the run-up to the bandh, with clashes reported between students and police in several districts. The Municipal Administration and Urban Development (MAUD) department sanctioned ₹77.31 crore to upgrade 404 traffic signals in Hyderabad.

---

## SECTION 15 — Andhra Pradesh State Updates
Union Defence Minister Rajnath Singh and Andhra Pradesh Governor S. Abdul Nazeer are scheduled to visit Visakhapatnam on July 10 for official engagements. Rajnath Singh will attend the commissioning ceremony of the Indian Navy's warship Mahendragiri at the Naval Dockyard on Saturday, July 11. The Andhra Pradesh government has granted in-principle approval for a ₹750 crore project aimed at developing Tirupati into a 'City of Lakes and Tanks' to enhance climate resilience and mitigate flood risks. Andhra Pradesh Civil Supplies Minister Nadendla Manohar announced that the state achieved its highest-ever paddy procurement of 7.4 million tonnes under the NDA coalition government's reforms, ensuring faster payments to farmers. Health Minister and NTR district in-charge Satya Kumar Yadav directed officials to accelerate the implementation of Chief Minister Chandrababu Naidu's assurances and complete projects within the stipulated timeframe, with a focus on infrastructure works in the Vijayawada parliamentary constituency. Officials of the Election Commission of India (ECI) reviewed the Special Intensive Revision (SIR) of electoral rolls in Visakhapatnam district on Thursday, July 9, including field visits and a review of the digitization process.

## SECTION 18 — Deep Analysis of Most Important Breaking News
**What Happened:** The United States has engaged in a third consecutive day of strikes against Iran, with reports of explosions in Bushehr and Konarak, following earlier strikes in response to alleged Iranian attacks on commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz. Iran has pledged a "crushing response" and retaliated with drone and missile attacks on U.S. military sites in Bahrain and Kuwait.
**Root Causes:** The immediate cause is the U.S. response to Iranian actions against commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, a critical chokepoint for global oil supplies. This escalation builds upon a long history of geopolitical friction between the two nations, exacerbated by the U.S. revocation of a sanctions waiver on Iranian oil sales.
**Historical Background:** Tensions between the U.S. and Iran have been a recurring feature of Middle East geopolitics for decades, often centered around Iran's nuclear program, regional influence, and control over strategic waterways like the Strait of Hormuz. Previous ceasefires and agreements have proven fragile, leading to cycles of escalation and de-escalation. The recent strikes follow a memorandum of understanding to end the war with Iran which the U.S. President has reportedly declared "over."
**Key Actors:** Key actors include the United States and its military command, the Iranian military and Revolutionary Guards, Bahrain and Kuwait (as sites of U.S. military presence), and international bodies like the UN, which is calling for de-escalation.
**Strategic Importance:** This escalating conflict in the Gulf holds immense strategic importance due to its potential impact on global oil supplies flowing through the Strait of Hormuz, which can trigger significant market instability and exacerbate global inflation. It also risks widening regional conflicts and further destabilizing the Middle East, drawing in more international actors.
**Possible Long-Term Consequences:** Long-term consequences could include sustained volatility in global energy markets, increased defense spending and military presence in the Middle East, a further entrenchment of regional rivalries, and potential for humanitarian crises if conflict broadens. It could also lead to a more fragmented international approach to Middle East security. This remains a developing story.

## SECTION 19 — This Day in History (Global)
*   In 1778, Louis XVI of France declared war on the Kingdom of Great Britain during the American Revolution.
*   The Vellore Mutiny, the first large-scale mutiny by Indian sepoys against the British East India Company, broke out in 1806.
*   In 1890, Wyoming was admitted as the 44th U.S. state.
*   The Battle of Britain began in 1940 as the German Luftwaffe launched attacks on southern England during World War II.
*   In 1947, Muhammad Ali Jinnah was recommended as the first Governor-General of Pakistan by British Prime Minister Clement Attlee.
*   Armistice negotiations to end the Korean War began at Kaesong in 1951.
*   Telstar, the world's first communications satellite, was launched into orbit in 1962, transmitting the first live television images.
*   New Zealand decimalized its currency to the modern-day New Zealand dollar in 1967.
*   The Bahamas gained full independence within the Commonwealth of Nations in 1973.
*   In 1985, the Greenpeace protest ship Rainbow Warrior was bombed and sunk in Auckland, New Zealand, by French intelligence agents.
*   Boris Yeltsin was sworn in as the first elected president of the Russian Federation in 1991.

---

## SECTION 20 — This Day in History (India)
*   The Vellore Mutiny, the first large-scale mutiny by Indian soldiers against the British, erupted in 1806.
*   Fort William College was founded by Lord Wellesley in 1800.
*   In 1947, the Indian Independence Bill was introduced.
*   Sunil Gavaskar, an Indian cricket commentator and former cricketer, was born on July 10, 1949.
*   Parveen Sultana, an Indian Hindustani classical singer, was born on July 10, 1950.
*   Rajnath Singh, currently India's Defence Minister, was born on July 10, 1951.
*   On July 10, 2017, eight Hindu civilian pilgrims were killed in a terror attack on their way from Amarnath Temple in Jammu and Kashmir.
*   Gopinath Bordoloi, a freedom fighter and the first Chief Minister of Assam, was born on July 10, 1890.
*   The death anniversary of Bhojpuri folk artist Bhikhari Thakur is observed on July 10.
